files.content:
# COMP 321 Project 2: Word Count

## Student Information
Partner 1 name and NetID: Not provided
Partner 2 name and NetID (if applicable): N/A

## Black Box Test Cases

1. Input: one file containing `Hello world\n` with flags `-c -w -l`.
Expected output counts: lines `1`, words `2`, chars `12`, plus a `total` line with the same values.
2. Input: three files `Banana.txt`, `apple.txt`, and `zebra.txt` with one line each, flags `-c -w -l`.
Expected behavior: outputs are sorted in ASCIIbetical order (`Banana.txt`, `apple.txt`, `zebra.txt`) and total is the sum of all three.
3. Input: one valid file and one missing file, flags `-c -w -l`.
Expected behavior: stderr contains `ERROR: cannot open file '<missing>'`, valid file still appears in stdout, and `total` only includes valid files.

## White Box Test Cases and Edge Cases

1. Empty file (`""`) with `-c -w -l`:
verifies EOF path, zero counts, and correct per-file plus total printing.
2. Blank-lines-only file (`"\n"` repeated many times) with `-c -w -l`:
verifies newline counting, zero-word behavior, and char count matching line count.
3. File with no trailing newline (`"no newline at end"`) with `-c -w -l`:
verifies line counting is based strictly on `'\n'`, while words/chars still count correctly.
4. Mixed whitespace (`spaces`, `tabs`, and multiple separators):
verifies word-boundary transitions using `isspace` and no double-counting across repeated whitespace.
5. Error-path ordering with multiple inputs:
verifies stderr errors are emitted in input order while successful stdout entries are sorted ASCIIbetically.

graderReview.content:
============================================================
COMP321 Word Count - LLM Grading Results
============================================================

RESULTS SUMMARY
---------------
Passed: 2/2 problems (70%)
Points: 7/10

DETAILED RESULTS
----------------
[PASS] Problem 1: Black Box Test Cases (3/5 pts)
       Your black-box tests show good practical understanding of core behavior (basic counting, ordering, and open-error handling), and the cases are concrete. The main issue is coverage: the rubric expects broader external-behavior testing across individual flags, flag combinations, and additional edge/input categories. To reach full credit, add several explicit cases with exact expected outputs, especially per-flag checks (`-c`, `-w`, `-l`), mixed flag combinations, and edge files (empty, whitespace-only, no trailing newline) plus additional input-source scenarios.
       Errors: Insufficient coverage breadth for full credit: only 3 tests instead of a broader suite.; Does not test flags independently or any flag combinations beyond `-c -w -l`.; Missing key black-box edge/input categories such as empty file, whitespace-only file, no-newline file, and stdin/no-file style input behavior.

[PASS] Problem 2: White Box Test Cases and Edge Cases (4/5 pts)
       This is a strong white-box section: the tests are concrete and tied to likely internal control flow and state transitions. You correctly target subtle logic around word boundaries and newline-based line counting. The main gap is low-level boundary/error-path depth expected for top marks: add tests that stress buffer boundaries and explicit read/permission failures, and mention resource cleanup verification. With those additions, this would be full-credit quality.
       Errors: No explicit buffer-boundary tests (large files, exact buffer-size multiples, boundary crossings).; Limited explicit coverage of read-failure/permission-denied paths and resource-management checks (e.g., file closure/leaks).

OVERALL COMMENTS
----------------
The submission shows solid understanding of expected `count` behavior and includes meaningful, concrete tests rather than vague statements. The strongest part is white-box reasoning about internal counting logic. The main pattern to improve is coverage breadth: add more flag-combination tests in black-box testing and deeper boundary/error-path stress tests (especially buffer-boundary and read/permission failures) in white-box testing.

============================================================
